description: This figure presents the engineering stress–strain curves of Fe–18Mn–10Al–xC low-density steels with three distinct carbon contents: 0.5C (black curve), 0.8C (red curve), and 1.2C (green curve). The x-axis represents engineering strain ranging from 0.0 to 0.6, while the y-axis shows engineering stress in MPa (0–1000 MPa). The 0.5C steel exhibits the lowest ductility with a total elongation (TE) of approximately 32.8% and an ultimate tensile strength (UTS) of ~857 MPa, failing at a strain of ~0.33. The 0.8C steel achieves the highest UTS of ~979 MPa with a TE of ~38.4%, demonstrating superior strength but intermediate ductility. The 1.2C steel displays the highest ductility with a TE of ~56.2% and a UTS of ~947 MPa, maintaining strain hardening to the highest strain level (~0.56). The curves demonstrate that increasing carbon content from 0.5% to 1.2% progressively enhances total elongation, while the UTS initially increases to a maximum at 0.8% C then slightly decreases at 1.2% C. This supports the core conclusion that the 1.2C steel achieves an exceptional strength–ductility combination, with a UTS × TE product of 53.2 GPa%, nearly double that of the 0.5C steel (28.1 GPa%), attributable to the fully austenitic microstructure containing uniformly distributed κ carbides and sustained strain hardening capacity.
